planting.note: Plant stem cuttings in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moisture-retentive soil in full sun; Daubenton's kale rarely sets viable seed and is propagated almost exclusively by stem cuttings, which root readily. The plant is truly perennial, regrowning from established roots each season and surviving indefinitely in frost-free conditions or with winter protection.

directions: Plant rooted stem cuttings in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moisture-retentive soil in full sun; Daubenton's kale is one of the oldest known perennial brassicas, grown continuously as a cutting-propagated vegetable in European kitchen gardens since at least the 18th century — named after the French naturalist and physician Louis Jean-Marie Daubenton (1716–1800). The plant never (or rarely) sets seed, remaining in a perpetual vegetative state and producing edible, tender, kale-like leaves throughout the growing season year after year. In zones 7–10, plants survive winter outdoors and return each spring with renewed vigor. Flavor is mild and pleasant, similar to curly kale but without the toughness of mature kale leaves. Harvest by cutting young side shoots and leaves regularly. An excellent plant for low-maintenance, productive food forests and permaculture gardens where a perennial harvest of leafy greens is desired. Propagate from 6-inch stem cuttings at any time.

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer or fall for fall/winter harvest in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; purple mustard greens are among the fastest cool-season crops, producing harvestable baby leaves in 3–4 weeks from sowing and full-sized leaves in 5–7 weeks. Bolts in heat and long days.

directions: Direct sow in late summer for fall harvest or very early spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; purple mustard greens (varieties include 'Red Giant', 'Osaka Purple', and 'Crimson Forest') produce large, crinkled or frilly, deep burgundy-red to purple-red leaves with a pleasant, pungent, spicy mustard flavor. The purple color is most intense in cool temperatures and fades slightly in warm weather. Extremely fast to harvest — baby leaves ready in 3–4 weeks, full size in 5–7 weeks. The flavor is spicier than milder Asian greens but milder than mature yellow mustard plants. Use in salad mixes for color and spice, stir-fry when young and tender, or braise the mature leaves Southern-style with pot liquor. Overwintering plants in zones 7–9 produce early spring regrowth before bolting. A staple of Asian-American vegetable gardens and increasingly popular in American farmers markets for its dramatic color and quick harvest.

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer for fall/winter harvest in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; swollen-stem mustard (zhacai type) requires a full cool season to develop the characteristic swollen basal stem that is the primary edible portion — it grows slowly in cool weather and will bolt without making a proper stem if planted too late.

directions: Direct sow in late summer for fall/winter harvest in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; gai choy (swollen-stem mustard, zhacai type) is the Chinese mustard grown for its enlarged, knobby, swollen basal stem that forms a distinctive corm-like structure — salted, dried, and fermented with chili and Sichuan pepper to produce 'zhacai' (榨菜), the pickled Sichuan preserved vegetable that flavors noodle soups, fried rice, and countless Chinese dishes. The peppery, pungent young leaves are also edible as cooking greens. Growing your own allows fresh stem harvest for home pickling or fresh use. Requires a long, cool growing season for the stem to swell to usable size — an autumn crop planted 10–12 weeks before frost in zones 7–9 works best. A fascinating vegetable for those interested in Asian fermentation and pickling traditions.

planting.note: Self-sows freely in garden beds, disturbed ground, and compost areas; if deliberately cultivating, direct sow in early spring or fall in average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade. Harvest young leaves before the plant bolts for mildest flavor.

directions: Common sow-thistle appears spontaneously in garden beds, compost areas, and disturbed ground across virtually all of North America — a Eurasian annual naturalized worldwide. The leaves are edible at all stages but mildest and most pleasant when harvested young (before the plant bolts), tasting somewhat like a milder, less bitter version of dandelion greens. Young rosette leaves are excellent in salads, stir-fries, or as a cooked pot herb — used in this way throughout the Mediterranean, China, and Africa as a common food plant. Despite the name and resemblance, it is in the Asteraceae family and not related to true thistles. The milky sap is harmless. Hares and rabbits eat the leaves avidly (hence 'hare's lettuce'). Recognized as a valuable foraging green in many food traditions. The daisy-like yellow flowers attract small native bees. Best harvested regularly as it appears in the garden, rather than deliberately cultivated in good beds where it would compete with food crops.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, rocky or sandy tropical soil in partial shade to full sun; black calabash grows naturally in tropical coastal hammocks and forest edges of Central America, the Caribbean, and southernmost Florida, tolerating shade and coastal conditions well.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average tropical soil in partial shade to full sun in zone 10–12; black calabash is a tropical tree of the Bignoniaceae family (the same family as trumpet creeper and catalpa) native to Central American and Caribbean coastal forests, producing unusual, large, round-to-oblong, gourd-like fruits with extremely hard, black shells when mature. The hard shells are used throughout Central America and the Caribbean to make maracas, rattles, cups, bowls, and other traditional crafts and musical instruments — the same traditional use as the unrelated calabash gourd (Lagenaria). The fleshy inner pulp is eaten by bats and birds, which disperse the seeds. The flowers, which appear directly on the trunk and main branches (cauliflory), are interesting botanical curiosities. Adapted to partial shade of tropical forest edges and hammocks. A rare conversation piece for south Florida collectors and native plant enthusiasts.
